title: Firearm

infobox.value: is any type of [gun](/wiki/Gun "Gun") that [shoots](/wiki/Shooting "Shooting") [projectiles](/wiki/Projectile "Projectile") using high [explosive](/wiki/Explosion "Explosion") pressure generated from [combustion](/wiki/Combustion "Combustion") ([deflagration](/wiki/Deflagration "Deflagration")) of chemical [propellant](/wiki/Propellant "Propellant"), most often [black powder](/wiki/Black_powder "Black powder") in [antique firearms](/wiki/Antique_firearms "Antique firearms") and [smokeless powder](/wiki/Smokeless_powder "Smokeless powder") in modern firearms. **Small arms** is a [subset](/wiki/Subset "Subset") of light firearms that is designed to be readily carried and operated by an individual.[[1]](#cite_note-Cole-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Merriam-webster.com-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Firearm-3) The term "firearm" is however variably defined in both technically and legally in different countries (see [legal definitions](#Legal_definitions)), and can be used colloquially (sometimes incorrectly) to refer to any type of guns.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Gun for an individual

This article is about the projectile weapon. For other uses, see [Firearm (disambiguation)](/wiki/Firearm_(disambiguation) "Firearm (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/M16_and_AK-47_comparison.png/500px-M16_and_AK-47_comparison.png)](/wiki/File:M16_and_AK-47_comparison.png)

The [M16 rifle](/wiki/M16_rifle "M16 rifle") and the [AK-47](/wiki/AK-47 "AK-47"), two common firearms with significant influences on firearm design

A **firearm** is any type of [gun](/wiki/Gun "Gun") that [shoots](/wiki/Shooting "Shooting") [projectiles](/wiki/Projectile "Projectile") using high [explosive](/wiki/Explosion "Explosion") pressure generated from [combustion](/wiki/Combustion "Combustion") ([deflagration](/wiki/Deflagration "Deflagration")) of chemical [propellant](/wiki/Propellant "Propellant"), most often [black powder](/wiki/Black_powder "Black powder") in [antique firearms](/wiki/Antique_firearms "Antique firearms") and [smokeless powder](/wiki/Smokeless_powder "Smokeless powder") in modern firearms. **Small arms** is a [subset](/wiki/Subset "Subset") of light firearms that is designed to be readily carried and operated by an individual.[[1]](#cite_note-Cole-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Merriam-webster.com-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Firearm-3) The term "firearm" is however variably defined in both technically and legally in different countries (see [legal definitions](#Legal_definitions)), and can be used colloquially (sometimes incorrectly) to refer to any type of guns.

The first firearms originated in 10th-century [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty") [China](/wiki/Medieval_China "Medieval China") (see [gunpowder weapons in the Song dynasty](/wiki/Gunpowder_weapons_in_the_Song_dynasty "Gunpowder weapons in the Song dynasty")), when [bamboo](/wiki/Bamboo "Bamboo") tubes containing [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder") and [pellet](/wiki/Shot_(pellet) "Shot (pellet)") projectiles were mounted on [spears](/wiki/Spear "Spear") to make the portable [fire lance](/wiki/Fire_lance "Fire lance"),[[4]](#cite_note-Helaine-4) which was operable by a single person and was later used effectively as a shock weapon in the [siege of De'an](/wiki/Siege_of_De%27an "Siege of De'an") in 1132. In the 13th century, fire lance barrels were replaced with metal tubes and transformed into the metal-barreled [hand cannon](/wiki/Hand_cannon "Hand cannon"),[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAndrade201652-5) and the technology gradually spread throughout [Eurasia](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia") during the 14th century. Older firearms typically used black powder as a [propellant](/wiki/Propellant "Propellant"), but modern firearms use [smokeless powder](/wiki/Smokeless_powder "Smokeless powder") or other explosive propellants. Most modern firearms (with the notable exception of [smoothbore](/wiki/Smoothbore "Smoothbore") [shotguns](/wiki/Shotgun "Shotgun")) have [rifled](/wiki/Rifling "Rifling") [barrels](/wiki/Gun_barrel "Gun barrel") to impart a stabilizing spin to the [bullet](/wiki/Bullet "Bullet") for improved [external ballistics](/wiki/External_ballistics "External ballistics").

Modern firearms can be described by their [caliber](/wiki/Caliber "Caliber") (i.e. [bore](/wiki/Gun_barrel#Bore "Gun barrel") [diameter](/wiki/Diameter "Diameter")). For [handguns](/wiki/Handgun "Handgun") and [rifles](/wiki/Rifle "Rifle") this is given in millimeters or inches (e.g. 7.62mm or .308 in.); in the case of [shotguns](/wiki/Shotgun "Shotgun"), [gauge or bore](/wiki/Gauge_(firearms) "Gauge (firearms)") (e.g. 12 ga. or .410 bore.). They are also described by the type of [action](/wiki/Action_(firearms) "Action (firearms)") employed ([single-shot](/wiki/Single-shot "Single-shot") vs. [repeating](/wiki/Repeating_firearm "Repeating firearm"), e.g. [muzzleloader](/wiki/Muzzleloader "Muzzleloader"), [breechloader](/wiki/Breechloader "Breechloader"), [lever](/wiki/Lever_action "Lever action"), [bolt](/wiki/Bolt_action "Bolt action"), [pump](/wiki/Pump_action "Pump action"), [revolver](/wiki/Revolver "Revolver"), [semi-automatic](/wiki/Semi-automatic_firearm "Semi-automatic firearm"), [fully automatic](/wiki/Automatic_firearm "Automatic firearm"), etc.), together with the usual means of deportment (i.e. handheld or [mounted](/wiki/Weapon_mount "Weapon mount")). Further classification may make reference to the type of barrel bore used (i.e. rifled vs smoothbore) and to the barrel length (e.g. 24 inches), to the firing mechanism (e.g. [matchlock](/wiki/Matchlock "Matchlock"), [flintlock](/wiki/Flintlock "Flintlock"), [wheellock](/wiki/Wheellock "Wheellock"), [caplock](/wiki/Caplock "Caplock") or [primer](/wiki/Primer_(firearms) "Primer (firearms)")-ignited such as [needlefire](/wiki/Needle_gun "Needle gun"), [pinfire](/wiki/Pinfire "Pinfire"), [teat-fire](/wiki/Teat-fire "Teat-fire"), [rimfire](/wiki/Rimfire_ammunition "Rimfire ammunition") and [centerfire](/wiki/Centerfire "Centerfire"), as well as the atypical [electronic firing](/wiki/Electronic_firing "Electronic firing")), to the design's primary intended use (e.g. [hunting](/wiki/Hunting "Hunting") rifle,
[service rifle](/wiki/Service_rifle "Service rifle")), or to the commonly accepted name for a particular variation (e.g. [Gatling gun](/wiki/Gatling_gun "Gatling gun")).

Shooters aim firearms at their targets with [hand-eye coordination](/wiki/Hand-eye_coordination "Hand-eye coordination"), using either [iron sights](/wiki/Iron_sights "Iron sights") or [optical sights](/wiki/Optical_sight "Optical sight") such as [scope](/wiki/Telescopic_sight "Telescopic sight"), [red dot](/wiki/Red_dot_sight "Red dot sight") or [holographic sights](/wiki/Holographic_sight "Holographic sight"). The [effective range](/wiki/Effective_range "Effective range") of handguns and shotguns generally does not exceed 100 metres (110 yd; 330 ft), while most [rifles](/wiki/Rifle "Rifle") are accurate to 500 metres (550 yd; 1,600 ft) using iron sights, or to longer ranges whilst using optical sights. Purpose-built [sniper rifles](/wiki/Sniper_rifle "Sniper rifle") and [anti-materiel rifles](/wiki/Anti-materiel_rifle "Anti-materiel rifle") are accurate to ranges of more than 2,000 metres (2,200 yd). Firearm rounds may be dangerous or lethal well beyond their accurate range; the minimum distance for safety is much greater than the specified range for accuracy.

## Types

For a more detailed list of common firearms, see [List of firearms](/wiki/List_of_firearms "List of firearms"), [List of most-produced firearms](/wiki/List_of_most-produced_firearms "List of most-produced firearms"), and [Small arms and light weapons](/wiki/Small_arms_and_light_weapons "Small arms and light weapons").

A firearm is a [barreled](/wiki/Gun_barrel "Gun barrel") weapon that inflicts damage on targets by launching one or more [projectiles](/wiki/Projectile "Projectile") driven by rapidly expanding high-pressure gas produced by [exothermic](/wiki/Exothermic_reaction "Exothermic reaction") combustion ([deflagration](/wiki/Deflagration "Deflagration")) of a chemical [propellant](/wiki/Propellant "Propellant"), historically [black powder](/wiki/Black_powder "Black powder"), now [smokeless powder](/wiki/Smokeless_powder "Smokeless powder").[[1]](#cite_note-Cole-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Merriam-webster.com-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Firearm-3)

In the military, firearms are categorized into *heavy* and *light* weapons by their portability. Light firearms are those that can be readily carried by individual [foot soldier](/wiki/Foot_soldier "Foot soldier"), though they might require more than one individual (i.e. [crew-served](/wiki/Crew-served_weapon "Crew-served weapon")) to achieve optimal operational capacity. Heavy firearms are those that are too large and heavy to be transported on foot, or too unstable against [recoil](/wiki/Recoil "Recoil"), and thus require the support of a [weapons platform](/wiki/Weapons_platform "Weapons platform") (e.g. a [fixed mount](/wiki/Weapon_mount#Static_mount "Weapon mount"), [wheeled carriage](/wiki/Gun_carriage "Gun carriage"), [vehicle](/wiki/Vehicle "Vehicle"), [aircraft](/wiki/Aircraft "Aircraft") or [water vessel](/wiki/Water_vessel "Water vessel")) to be tactically [mobile](/wiki/Mobility_(military) "Mobility (military)") or useful.

The [subset](/wiki/Subset "Subset") of light firearms that only use [kinetic projectiles](/wiki/Projectile#Kinetic_projectiles "Projectile") and are compact enough to be operated to full capacity by a single infantryman (individual-served) are also referred to as **small arms**. Such firearms include [handguns](/wiki/Handgun "Handgun") such as [pistols](/wiki/Pistol "Pistol"), [revolvers](/wiki/Revolver "Revolver"), and [derringers](/wiki/Derringer "Derringer"); and [long guns](/wiki/Long_gun "Long gun") such as [rifles](/wiki/Rifle "Rifle") (and their subtypes), [shotguns](/wiki/Shotgun "Shotgun"), [submachine guns](/wiki/Submachine_gun "Submachine gun"), and [light machine guns](/wiki/Light_machine_gun "Light machine gun").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Firearm&action=edit) the [Small Arms Survey](/wiki/Small_Arms_Survey "Small Arms Survey") reported that there were over one billion firearms distributed globally, of which 857 million (about 85 percent) were in [civilian](/wiki/Civilian "Civilian") hands.[[14]](#cite_note-smallarmssurvey.org-14)[[15]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-15) U.S. civilians alone account for 393 million (about 46 percent) of the worldwide total of civilian-held firearms,[[15]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-15) as of 2026 the number of civilian-held firearms is estimated to be as much as 500 million.[[16]](#cite_note-Ammo-16) This amounts to "120.5 firearms for every 100 residents",[[15]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-15) which increased to 150 guns per 100 residents by 2026.[[16]](#cite_note-Ammo-16) The world's [armed forces](/wiki/Armed_forces "Armed forces") control about 133 million (about 13 percent) of the global total of small arms, of which over 43 percent belong to two countries: the [Russian Federation](/wiki/Russian_Federation "Russian Federation") (30.3 million) and [China](/wiki/China "China") (27.5 million).[[14]](#cite_note-smallarmssurvey.org-14) [Law enforcement agencies](/wiki/Law_enforcement_agency "Law enforcement agency") control about 23 million (about 2 percent) of the global total of small arms.[[14]](#cite_note-smallarmssurvey.org-14)

### Handguns

Main article: [Handgun](/wiki/Handgun "Handgun")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Colt_Autentica.jpg/250px-Colt_Autentica.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colt_Autentica.jpg)

The [Colt Single Action Army](/wiki/Colt_Single_Action_Army "Colt Single Action Army"), a revolver chambered in [.45 Colt](/wiki/.45_Colt ".45 Colt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Glock_19_Gen_4_pistol.JPG/250px-Glock_19_Gen_4_pistol.JPG)](/wiki/File:Glock_19_Gen_4_pistol.JPG)

The [Glock 19](/wiki/Glock_19 "Glock 19"), a pistol chambered in [9×19mm Parabellum](/wiki/9%C3%9719mm_Parabellum "9×19mm Parabellum")

A handgun is, as defined generally and in many [gun laws](/wiki/Gun_laws "Gun laws"), a firearm that can be used with a single hand.[[17]](#cite_note-act60of2000-17)[[18]](#cite_note-www5.austlii.edu.au-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) They are the smallest of all firearms, and are common as [sidearms](/wiki/Sidearm_(weapon) "Sidearm (weapon)"), [concealed carry](/wiki/Concealed_carry "Concealed carry") weapons, or as backup weapons for [self-defense](/wiki/Self-defense "Self-defense").

Handguns can be categorized into two broad types: [pistols](/wiki/Pistol "Pistol"), which have a single fixed firing chamber machined into the rear of the barrel, and are often loaded using magazines of varying capacities; [revolvers](/wiki/Revolver "Revolver"), which have a number of firing chambers or "charge holes" in a [revolving cylinder](/wiki/Cylinder_(firearms) "Cylinder (firearms)"), each one loaded with a single [cartridge](/wiki/Cartridge_(firearms) "Cartridge (firearms)") or charge; and [derringers](/wiki/Derringer "Derringer"), broadly defined as any handgun that is not a traditional pistol nor a revolver.[[21]](#cite_note-Handgun_types-21)

There are various types of the aforementioned handguns designed for different mechanisms or purposes, such as [single-shot](/wiki/Single-shot "Single-shot"), [manual repeating](/wiki/Lever_action "Lever action"), [semi-automatic](/wiki/Semi-automatic_pistol "Semi-automatic pistol"), or [automatic](/wiki/Machine_pistol "Machine pistol") pistols; [single-action](/wiki/Single-action "Single-action"), [double-action](/wiki/Trigger_(firearms)#Double-action_only "Trigger (firearms)"), or [double-action/single-action](/wiki/Trigger_(firearms)#Double-action/single-action "Trigger (firearms)") revolvers; and small, compact handguns for concealed carry such as [pocket pistols](/wiki/Pocket_pistol "Pocket pistol") and "[Saturday night specials](/wiki/Saturday_night_special "Saturday night special")".[[22]](#cite_note-USCCA-22)

Examples of pistols include [Glocks](/wiki/Glock "Glock"), [Browning Hi-Power](/wiki/Browning_Hi-Power "Browning Hi-Power"), [M1911 pistol](/wiki/M1911_pistol "M1911 pistol"), [Makarov pistol](/wiki/Makarov_pistol "Makarov pistol"), [Walther PP](/wiki/Walther_PP "Walther PP"), [Luger pistol](/wiki/Luger_pistol "Luger pistol"), [Mauser C96](/wiki/Mauser_C96 "Mauser C96"), and [Beretta 92](/wiki/Beretta_92 "Beretta 92"). Examples of revolvers include the [Colt Single Action Army](/wiki/Colt_Single_Action_Army "Colt Single Action Army"), [Smith & Wesson Model 10](/wiki/Smith_%26_Wesson_Model_10 "Smith & Wesson Model 10"), [Colt Official Police](/wiki/Colt_Official_Police "Colt Official Police"), [Colt Python](/wiki/Colt_Python "Colt Python"), [New Nambu M60](/wiki/New_Nambu_M60 "New Nambu M60"), and [Mateba Autorevolver](/wiki/Mateba_Autorevolver "Mateba Autorevolver"). Examples of derringers include the [Remington Model 95](/wiki/Remington_Model_95 "Remington Model 95"), [FP-45 Liberator](/wiki/FP-45_Liberator "FP-45 Liberator"), and [COP .357 Derringer](/wiki/COP_.357_Derringer "COP .357 Derringer").[[22]](#cite_note-USCCA-22)[[23]](#cite_note-Pew_Pew_Handgun-23)

### Long guns

Main article: [Long gun](/wiki/Long_gun "Long gun")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Capture_of_English_long-barreled_guns_in_the_breakthrough_battle_at_Bapaume%2C_March_1918.png/250px-Capture_of_English_long-barreled_guns_in_the_breakthrough_battle_at_Bapaume%2C_March_1918.png)](/wiki/File:Capture_of_English_long-barreled_guns_in_the_breakthrough_battle_at_Bapaume,_March_1918.png)

Capture of English long-barreled guns in the breakthrough battle at Bapaume, March 1918

A long gun is any firearm with a notably long barrel, typically a length of 10 to 30 inches (250 to 760 mm) (there are restrictions on minimum barrel length in many jurisdictions; maximum barrel length is usually a matter of practicality). Unlike a handgun, long guns are designed to be held and fired with both hands, while braced against the shoulder for better stability. The receiver and trigger group is mounted into a stock made of wood, plastic, metal, or composite material, which has sections that form a foregrip, rear grip, and optionally (but typically) a shoulder mount called the *butt*.[[24]](#cite_note-NIJ_Long_gun-24) Early long arms, from the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance") up to the mid-19th century, were generally smoothbore firearms that fired one or more ball shot, called [muskets](/wiki/Musket "Musket") or [arquebuses](/wiki/Arquebus "Arquebus") depending on caliber and firing mechanism. Since the 19th and 20th centuries, various types of long guns have been created for different purposes.

#### Rifles

Main article: [Rifle](/wiki/Rifle "Rifle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Bastogne_War_Museum_Mosin-Nagant_Model_1944_27-02-2021_11-13-43.jpg/250px-Bastogne_War_Museum_Mosin-Nagant_Model_1944_27-02-2021_11-13-43.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bastogne_War_Museum_Mosin-Nagant_Model_1944_27-02-2021_11-13-43.jpg)

The [Mosin–Nagant](/wiki/Mosin%E2%80%93Nagant "Mosin–Nagant"), a rifle chambered in [7.62×54mmR](/wiki/7.62%C3%9754mmR "7.62×54mmR")

A rifle is a long gun that has [riflings](/wiki/Rifling "Rifling") (spiral grooves) machined into the [bore](/wiki/Gauge_(firearms) "Gauge (firearms)") (inner) surface of its barrel, imparting a gyroscopically stabilizing spin to the [bullets](/wiki/Bullet "Bullet") that it fires. A descendant of the musket, rifles produce a single point of impact with each firing with a long range and high accuracy. For this reason, as well as for their ubiquity, rifles are very popular among militaries as [service rifles](/wiki/Service_rifle "Service rifle"), police as accurate long-range alternatives to their traditional [shotgun](/wiki/Shotgun "Shotgun") long guns, and civilians for [hunting](/wiki/Hunting "Hunting"), [shooting sports](/wiki/Shooting_sports "Shooting sports"), and [self-defense](/wiki/Self-defense "Self-defense").[[24]](#cite_note-NIJ_Long_gun-24)

Many types of rifles exist owing to their wide adoption and versatility, ranging from mere barrel length differences as in [short-barreled rifles](/wiki/Short-barreled_rifle "Short-barreled rifle") and [carbines](/wiki/Carbine "Carbine"), to classifications per the rifle's function and purpose as in [semi-automatic rifles](/wiki/Semi-automatic_rifle "Semi-automatic rifle"), [automatic rifles](/wiki/Automatic_rifle "Automatic rifle") and [sniper rifles](/wiki/Sniper_rifle "Sniper rifle"), to differences in the rifle's action as in [falling block](/wiki/Falling-block_action "Falling-block action"), [rolling block](/wiki/Rolling_block "Rolling block"), [single-shot](/wiki/Single-shot "Single-shot"), [break-action](/wiki/Break-action "Break-action"), [bolt-action](/wiki/Bolt-action "Bolt-action"), and [lever-action](/wiki/Lever-action "Lever-action") rifles.[[24]](#cite_note-NIJ_Long_gun-24)

Examples of rifles of various types include the [Henry rifle](/wiki/Henry_rifle "Henry rifle"), [Winchester Model 94](/wiki/Winchester_Model_94 "Winchester Model 94"), [Winchester Model 70](/wiki/Winchester_Model_70 "Winchester Model 70"), [Lee–Enfield](/wiki/Lee%E2%80%93Enfield "Lee–Enfield"), [Gewehr 98](/wiki/Gewehr_98 "Gewehr 98"), [M1 Garand](/wiki/M1_Garand "M1 Garand"), [AK-type](/wiki/Kalashnikov_rifle "Kalashnikov rifle"), [AR-15](/wiki/AR-15%E2%80%93style_rifle "AR-15–style rifle"), [Ruger 10/22](/wiki/Ruger_10/22 "Ruger 10/22"), [Ruger M77](/wiki/Ruger_M77 "Ruger M77"), [Ruger Mini-14](/wiki/Ruger_Mini-14 "Ruger Mini-14"), [Heckler & Koch G3](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_G3 "Heckler & Koch G3"), [Remington Model 700](/wiki/Remington_Model_700 "Remington Model 700"), [Remington Model 7600](/wiki/Remington_Model_7600 "Remington Model 7600"), and [Heckler & Koch HK417](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_HK417 "Heckler & Koch HK417").[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

#### Shotguns

Main article: [Shotgun](/wiki/Shotgun "Shotgun")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Mossberg_500a_1.jpg/250px-Mossberg_500a_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mossberg_500a_1.jpg)

The [Mossberg 500](/wiki/Mossberg_500 "Mossberg 500"), a shotgun chambered in [12-gauge](/wiki/12-gauge "12-gauge")

A shotgun is a long gun that has a predominantly [smoothbore](/wiki/Smoothbore "Smoothbore") barrel—meaning it lacks [rifling](/wiki/Rifling "Rifling")—designed to fire a number of [shot pellets](/wiki/Shot_(pellet) "Shot (pellet)") in each discharge.[[27]](#cite_note-18_USC_Shotgun-27) These shot pellet sizes commonly range between 2 mm #9 [birdshot](/wiki/Shotgun_cartridge#Birdshot "Shotgun cartridge") and 8.4 mm #00 (double-aught) [buckshot](/wiki/Shotgun_cartridge#Buckshot "Shotgun cartridge"), and produce a cluster of impact points with considerably less range and accuracy compared to a rifle, since [shot spreads](/wiki/Shotgun_shell#Spread "Shotgun shell") during flight. Shotguns are also capable of firing single solid projectiles called [slugs](/wiki/Shotgun_slug "Shotgun slug") (typically used for [hunting large animals](/wiki/Big-game_hunting "Big-game hunting")), or specialty (often "[less lethal](/wiki/Non-lethal_weapon "Non-lethal weapon")") munitions such as [bean bags](/wiki/Bean_bag_round "Bean bag round") or [tear gas](/wiki/Tear_gas "Tear gas") to function as a [riot gun](/wiki/Riot_gun "Riot gun"), or [breaching rounds](/wiki/Breaching_round "Breaching round") to function as a [door breaching](/wiki/Door_breaching "Door breaching") shotgun. Shotgun munitions, regardless of type, are packed into [shotgun shells](/wiki/Shotgun_shell "Shotgun shell") (cartridges designed specifically for shotguns) that are loaded into the shotgun for use; these shells are commonly loose and manually loaded one-by-one, though some shotguns accept magazines.[[28]](#cite_note-NRA_shotshell-28)

Shotguns share many qualities with rifles, such as both being descendants of early long guns such as the musket; both having [single-shot](/wiki/Single-shot "Single-shot"), [break-action](/wiki/Break-action "Break-action"), [bolt-action](/wiki/Bolt-action "Bolt-action"), [lever-action](/wiki/Lever-action "Lever-action"), [pump-action](/wiki/Pump-action "Pump-action"), [semi-automatic](/wiki/Semi-automatic_shotgun "Semi-automatic shotgun"), and [automatic](/wiki/Automatic_shotgun "Automatic shotgun") variants;[[29]](#cite_note-Shotgun_types-29) and both being popular with military, police, and civilians for largely the same reasons.[[24]](#cite_note-NIJ_Long_gun-24) However, unlike rifles, shotguns are less favored in combat roles due to their low accuracy and limited effectiveness in [modern warfare](/wiki/Modern_warfare "Modern warfare"), with [combat shotguns](/wiki/Combat_shotgun "Combat shotgun") often only used for [breaching](/wiki/Door_breaching "Door breaching"), or [close-quarters combat](/wiki/Close-quarters_combat "Close-quarters combat") situations like [jungle warfare](/wiki/Jungle_warfare "Jungle warfare"), [trench warfare](/wiki/Trench_warfare "Trench warfare"), or clearing buildings during [urban warfare](/wiki/Urban_warfare "Urban warfare"); for versatility underbarrel attachments such as the [M26 Modular Accessory Shotgun System](/wiki/M26_Modular_Accessory_Shotgun_System "M26 Modular Accessory Shotgun System") are popular in the military, as they allow a soldier to wield a shotgun and an assault rifle simultaneously.[[30]](#cite_note-Shotgun_military-30) Shotguns are still popular with civilians for the suitability of their shot spread in hunting, [clay pigeon shooting](/wiki/Clay_pigeon_shooting "Clay pigeon shooting"), and home defense.

[Double-barreled shotguns](/wiki/Double-barreled_shotgun "Double-barreled shotgun") are break-action shotguns with two parallel barrels (horizontal side-by-side or vertical over-and-under), allowing two single shots that can be loaded and fired in quick succession.

Examples of shotguns include the [Winchester Model 1897](/wiki/Winchester_Model_1897 "Winchester Model 1897"), [Winchester SXP](/wiki/Winchester_Model_1200 "Winchester Model 1200"), [Browning Auto-5](/wiki/Browning_Auto-5 "Browning Auto-5"), [Ithaca 37](/wiki/Ithaca_37 "Ithaca 37"), [Remington Model 870](/wiki/Remington_Model_870 "Remington Model 870"), [Mossberg 500](/wiki/Mossberg_500 "Mossberg 500"), [Benelli M4](/wiki/Benelli_M4 "Benelli M4"), [Franchi SPAS-12](/wiki/Franchi_SPAS-12 "Franchi SPAS-12"), [Atchisson AA-12](/wiki/Atchisson_AA-12 "Atchisson AA-12").[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

#### Carbines

Main article: [Carbine](/wiki/Carbine "Carbine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/M1_Carbine_Mk_I_-_USA_-_Arm%C3%A9museum_noBG_croped.jpg/250px-M1_Carbine_Mk_I_-_USA_-_Arm%C3%A9museum_noBG_croped.jpg)](/wiki/File:M1_Carbine_Mk_I_-_USA_-_Arm%C3%A9museum_noBG_croped.jpg)

The [M1 carbine](/wiki/M1_carbine "M1 carbine"), a uniquely-designed carbine, chambered in [.30 carbine](/wiki/.30_carbine ".30 carbine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Coltm4a1.jpeg/250px-Coltm4a1.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Coltm4a1.jpeg)

The [M4 carbine](/wiki/M4_carbine "M4 carbine"), a carbine derivative of the [M16 rifle](/wiki/M16_rifle "M16 rifle"), chambered in [5.56×45mm NATO](/wiki/5.56%C3%9745mm_NATO "5.56×45mm NATO")

The traditional definition of a carbine was a shortened version of an existing rifle, the term is derived from the French word for rifleman, “carabinier”.[[33]](#cite_note-Carbine_F&S-33) A carbine was originally defined as a rifle with a barrel under 22 inches (56 cm), which was shorter than the common military rifle barrel lengths of 23 in (58 cm) to 26 in (66 cm) from the late 18th-early 20th centuries. One of the first carbines to be used by the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") was the [Spencer carbine](/wiki/Spencer_repeating_rifle "Spencer repeating rifle") adopted in 1860, it had a 22 inch barrel and was designed to be more quickly drawn from a [saddle](/wiki/Saddle "Saddle") [scabbard](/wiki/Scabbard "Scabbard").[[34]](#cite_note-USCCA_carbine-34) During the late 19th and early 20th century, [U.S. Army Cavalry](/wiki/United_States_Cavalry "United States Cavalry") carried carbine conversions of lever action rifles like the [Winchester Model 1873 Carbine](/wiki/Winchester_rifle#Model_1873 "Winchester rifle"), which had the standard 26 in (66 cm) barrel shortened to 20 in (51 cm). The definition of a carbine evolved and changed somewhat in the 20th century, especially in the United States after the passage of the [National Firearms Act](/wiki/National_Firearms_Act "National Firearms Act") (NFA) in 1934. The NFA created the term [short-barreled rifle](/wiki/Short-barreled_rifle "Short-barreled rifle") (SBR), which is defined as a rifle with a barrel length under 16 in (41 cm) or with an overall length under 26 in (66 cm),[[35]](#cite_note-35) under the NFA possession of an *SBR* is a class D felony unless the [ATF](/wiki/Bureau_of_Alcohol,_Tobacco,_Firearms_and_Explosives "Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives") issues a type of permit known as a NFA tax stamp.[[36]](#cite_note-NFA-36) This led the international firearms industry (for the most part) to adopt the definition of a carbine as being any rifle with a barrel length under 20 in (51 cm), and in the U.S. carbine has come to mean any rifle with a barrel length between 20-16 inches, and while rifles with barrels under 16 inches are still defined as a carbine, they're usually called an *SBR* for clarification.[[34]](#cite_note-USCCA_carbine-34) Not all carbines are shortened versions of existing rifles, for example the [M1 carbine](/wiki/M1_carbine "M1 carbine") was designed with a 17.75 in (45.1 cm) barrel and [Ruger Mini-14](/wiki/Ruger_Mini-14 "Ruger Mini-14") models have either 18.5 in (47 cm) or 16.1 in (41 cm) barrels.[[34]](#cite_note-USCCA_carbine-34)[[37]](#cite_note-37) Carbines are regardless very similar to rifles and often have the same actions (single-shot, lever-action, bolt-action, semi-automatic, automatic, etc.).

The small size of a carbine provides lighter weight and better maneuverability, making them ideal for [close-quarters combat](/wiki/Close-quarters_combat "Close-quarters combat") and storage in compact areas. This makes them popular firearms among [special forces](/wiki/Special_forces "Special forces") and [police tactical units](/wiki/Police_tactical_unit "Police tactical unit") alongside [submachine guns](/wiki/Submachine_gun "Submachine gun"), considerably so since the late 1990s due to the familiarity and better [stopping power](/wiki/Stopping_power "Stopping power") of carbines compared to submachine guns. They are also popular with (and were originally mostly intended for) military personnel in roles that are expected to engage in combat, but where a full-size rifle would be an impediment to the primary duties of that soldier ([logistical](/wiki/Military_logistics "Military logistics") personnel, [airborne forces](/wiki/Airborne_forces "Airborne forces"), [military engineers](/wiki/Military_engineers "Military engineers"), [officers](/wiki/Officer_(armed_forces) "Officer (armed forces)"), etc.), though since the turn of the millennium these have been superseded to a degree in some roles by [personal defense weapons](/wiki/Personal_defense_weapon "Personal defense weapon"). Carbines are also common among civilian firearm owners who have size, space, and power concerns similar to military and police users.[[34]](#cite_note-USCCA_carbine-34)

Examples of carbines include the [Winchester Model 1892](/wiki/Winchester_Model_1892 "Winchester Model 1892"), [Rifle No. 5 Mk I](/wiki/Rifle_No._5_Mk_I "Rifle No. 5 Mk I"), [SKS](/wiki/SKS "SKS"), [M1 carbine](/wiki/M1_carbine "M1 carbine"), [Ruger Mini-14](/wiki/Ruger_Mini-14 "Ruger Mini-14"), [M4 carbine](/wiki/M4_carbine "M4 carbine"), [Colt AR-15](/wiki/Colt_AR-15 "Colt AR-15"), and [Kel-Tec SUB-2000](/wiki/Kel-Tec_SUB-2000 "Kel-Tec SUB-2000").[[34]](#cite_note-USCCA_carbine-34)

#### Assault rifles

Main article: [Assault rifle](/wiki/Assault_rifle "Assault rifle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Kalaschnikow_AK_107_noBG.png/250px-Kalaschnikow_AK_107_noBG.png)](/wiki/File:Kalaschnikow_AK_107_noBG.png)

The [AK-107](/wiki/AK-107 "AK-107"), an assault rifle chambered in [5.45×39mm](/wiki/5.45%C3%9739mm "5.45×39mm")

An assault rifle is commonly defined as a [selective-fire](/wiki/Selective_fire "Selective fire") rifle chambered in an [intermediate cartridge](/wiki/Intermediate_cartridge "Intermediate cartridge") (such as [5.56×45mm NATO](/wiki/5.56%C3%9745mm_NATO "5.56×45mm NATO"), [7.62×39mm](/wiki/7.62%C3%9739mm "7.62×39mm"), [5.45×39mm](/wiki/5.45%C3%9739mm "5.45×39mm"), and [.300 AAC Blackout](/wiki/.300_AAC_Blackout ".300 AAC Blackout")) and fed with a detachable [magazine](/wiki/Magazine_(firearms) "Magazine (firearms)").[[38]](#cite_note-BritanicaDef-38)[[39]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-39)[[40]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-40)[[41]](#cite_note-autogenerated4-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Musgave,_Daniel_D._1967-42) Assault rifles are also usually smaller than full-sized rifles such as [battle rifles](/wiki/Battle_rifle "Battle rifle").

Originating with the [StG 44](/wiki/StG_44 "StG 44") produced by [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), assault rifles have since become extremely popular among militaries and other armed groups due to their universal versatility, and they have made up the vast majority of standard-issue military [service rifles](/wiki/Service_rifle "Service rifle") since the mid-20th century.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Various configurations of assault rifle exist, such as the [bullpup](/wiki/Bullpup "Bullpup"), in which the firing grip is located in front of the [breech](/wiki/Chamber_(firearms) "Chamber (firearms)") instead of behind it.

Examples of assault rifles include the [Kalashnikov rifles](/wiki/Kalashnikov_rifle "Kalashnikov rifle") of Soviet and Russian origin (such as the [AK-47](/wiki/AK-47 "AK-47"), [AKM](/wiki/AKM "AKM"), and [AK-74](/wiki/AK-74 "AK-74")), as well as the American [M4 carbine](/wiki/M4_carbine "M4 carbine") and [M16 rifle](/wiki/M16_rifle "M16 rifle").

#### Battle rifles

Main article: [Battle rifle](/wiki/Battle_rifle "Battle rifle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/FN_FAL_compilation.jpg/250px-FN_FAL_compilation.jpg)](/wiki/File:FN_FAL_compilation.jpg)

The [FN FAL](/wiki/FN_FAL "FN FAL"), a battle rifle chambered in [7.62×51mm NATO](/wiki/7.62%C3%9751mm_NATO "7.62×51mm NATO")

A battle rifle is commonly defined as a semi-automatic or selective-fire rifle that is larger or longer than an assault rifle and is chambered in a "full-power" cartridge (e.g. [7.62×51mm NATO](/wiki/7.62%C3%9751mm_NATO "7.62×51mm NATO"), [7.92×57mm Mauser](/wiki/7.92%C3%9757mm_Mauser "7.92×57mm Mauser"), [7.62×54mmR](/wiki/7.62%C3%9754mmR "7.62×54mmR"), [6.8x51mm](/wiki/.277_Fury ".277 Fury")). The term originated as a [retronym](/wiki/Retronym "Retronym") to differentiate older full-powered rifles of these configurations like the [M1 Garand](/wiki/M1_Garand "M1 Garand") and [M14](/wiki/M14_Rifle "M14 Rifle"), from newer assault rifles using [intermediate cartridges](/wiki/Intermediate_cartridge "Intermediate cartridge") like the [Heckler & Koch HK33](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_HK33 "Heckler & Koch HK33") and [M16](/wiki/M16 "M16"), but it is sometimes used to describe similar modern rifles such as the [FN SCAR](/wiki/FN_SCAR "FN SCAR").[[44]](#cite_note-Battle_Rifle-44)

Battle rifles serve similar purposes as assault rifles, as they both are usually employed by ground infantry for essentially the same purposes. However, some prefer battle rifles for their more powerful cartridge, despite the added recoil. Some designated marksman rifles are configured from battle rifles, such as the [Mk 14 Enhanced Battle Rifle](/wiki/Mk_14_Enhanced_Battle_Rifle "Mk 14 Enhanced Battle Rifle") and [United States Marine Corps Designated Marksman Rifle](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps_Designated_Marksman_Rifle "United States Marine Corps Designated Marksman Rifle"), both essentially heavily modified and modernized variants of the [M14 rifle](/wiki/M14_rifle "M14 rifle").

Examples of rifles considered to be battle rifles include the [M1 Garand](/wiki/M1_Garand "M1 Garand"), [M14](/wiki/M14_Rifle "M14 Rifle"), [AR-10](/wiki/AR-10 "AR-10"), [FG 42](/wiki/FG_42 "FG 42"), [Gewehr 43](/wiki/Gewehr_43 "Gewehr 43"), [FN FAL](/wiki/FN_FAL "FN FAL"), [FN SCAR](/wiki/FN_SCAR "FN SCAR"), [SIG MCX Spear](/wiki/SIG_MCX_Spear "SIG MCX Spear"), [Howa Type 64](/wiki/Howa_Type_64 "Howa Type 64"), and [AK-308](/wiki/AK-12#AK-308 "AK-12").[[44]](#cite_note-Battle_Rifle-44)

#### Sniper rifles

Main article: [Sniper rifle](/wiki/Sniper_rifle "Sniper rifle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/G22_ohne_Schalldaempfer.jpg/330px-G22_ohne_Schalldaempfer.jpg)](/wiki/File:G22_ohne_Schalldaempfer.jpg)

The [Accuracy International Arctic Warfare](/wiki/Accuracy_International_Arctic_Warfare "Accuracy International Arctic Warfare"), a sniper rifle chambered in [7.62×51mm NATO](/wiki/7.62%C3%9751mm_NATO "7.62×51mm NATO") and [.308 Winchester](/wiki/.308_Winchester ".308 Winchester")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/M107_1.jpg/250px-M107_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:M107_1.jpg)

The [Barrett M82](/wiki/Barrett_M82 "Barrett M82"), an anti-materiel rifle chambered in [.50 BMG](/wiki/.50_BMG ".50 BMG")

A sniper rifle is, per widespread definition, a high-powered precision rifle, often [bolt-action](/wiki/Bolt-action "Bolt-action") or semi-automatic, with an [effective range](/wiki/Effective_range "Effective range") beyond that of a standard rifle, typically beyond 300 metres (330 yd).[[45]](#cite_note-Sniper-45) Though any rifle in a sniper configuration (usually with a [telescopic sight](/wiki/Telescopic_sight "Telescopic sight") and [bipod](/wiki/Bipod "Bipod")) can be considered a sniper rifle, most sniper rifles are purpose-built for their applications, or are variants of existing rifles that have been modified to function as sniper rifles, such as the [M1903A1/Unertl](/wiki/M1903_Springfield#Sniper_rifle "M1903 Springfield"), which was the [United States Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") sniper rifle configuration of the [M1903 Springfield](/wiki/M1903_Springfield "M1903 Springfield");[[46]](#cite_note-M1903A1-46) or the [Type 97 sniper rifle](/wiki/Type_97_sniper_rifle "Type 97 sniper rifle") used by the [Imperial Japanese Army](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Army "Imperial Japanese Army"), which was essentially a standard [Type 38 rifle](/wiki/Type_38_rifle "Type 38 rifle") that was modified to be lighter and come with a telescopic sight.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

Related developments are [anti-materiel rifles](/wiki/Anti-materiel_rifle "Anti-materiel rifle"), large-caliber rifles typically between 12.7 mm (0.50 in) to 14.5 mm (0.57 in) (sometimes larger)-i.e. [.50 BMG](/wiki/.50_BMG ".50 BMG") (12.7x99mm NATO), [12.7 × 108 mm](/wiki/12.7_%C3%97_108_mm "12.7 × 108 mm"), [20x102mm](/wiki/20_mm_caliber "20 mm caliber")-in calibers designed to destroy enemy [materiel](/wiki/Materiel "Materiel") such as vehicles, supplies, or hardware; anti-material rifles are also used as extreme long-range sniper rifles for distances of 1,000 metres (1,100 yd) to 2,000 m (2,200 yd).[[48]](#cite_note-Anti-material-48) [Anti-tank rifles](/wiki/Anti-tank_rifle "Anti-tank rifle") are anti-materiel rifles that were designed specifically to combat early [armored fighting vehicles](/wiki/Armoured_fighting_vehicle "Armoured fighting vehicle"), but are now largely obsolete due to advances in [vehicle armour](/wiki/Vehicle_armour "Vehicle armour").[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) [Scout sniper rifles](/wiki/Scout_rifle "Scout rifle") are a broad class of rifles generally summed up as short, lightweight, portable sniper rifles used by [Scout Snipers](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps_Scout_Sniper "United States Marine Corps Scout Sniper").[[51]](#cite_note-51) A [designated marksman rifle](/wiki/Designated_marksman_rifle "Designated marksman rifle") is a semi-automatic high-precision rifle, they're usually chambered in intermediate or full-power cartridges like the [7.62x51mm NATO](/wiki/7.62x51mm_NATO "7.62x51mm NATO"), and are designed to fill the range gap between sniper rifles and regular infantry rifles, they are designed for engagements beyond 300 yards (270 m) with a maximum range of 800 yards (730 m), and are wielded by [designated marksmen](/wiki/Designated_marksmen "Designated marksmen").[[52]](#cite_note-52)

Examples of sniper and scout rifles include the [M40 rifle](/wiki/M40_rifle "M40 rifle"), [Heckler & Koch PSG1](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_PSG1 "Heckler & Koch PSG1"), [Walther WA 2000](/wiki/Walther_WA_2000 "Walther WA 2000"), [Accuracy International AWM](/wiki/Accuracy_International_AWM "Accuracy International AWM"), [M24 Sniper Weapon System](/wiki/M24_Sniper_Weapon_System "M24 Sniper Weapon System"), [Mk 22 MOD 0 Advanced Sniper Rifle](/wiki/Barrett_MRAD "Barrett MRAD"), [Steyr Scout](/wiki/Steyr_Scout "Steyr Scout"), [Sako TRG](/wiki/Sako_TRG "Sako TRG"), and [CheyTac Intervention](/wiki/CheyTac_Intervention "CheyTac Intervention"). Examples of anti-materiel and anti-tank rifles include the [Mauser Tankgewehr M1918](/wiki/Mauser_Tankgewehr_M1918 "Mauser Tankgewehr M1918"), [Boys anti-tank rifle](/wiki/Boys_anti-tank_rifle "Boys anti-tank rifle"), [Lahti L-39](/wiki/Lahti_L-39 "Lahti L-39"), [PTRS-41](/wiki/PTRS-41 "PTRS-41"), [Barrett M82](/wiki/Barrett_M82 "Barrett M82"), [Gepárd anti-materiel rifle](/wiki/Gep%C3%A1rd_anti-materiel_rifle "Gepárd anti-materiel rifle"), [McMillan TAC-50](/wiki/McMillan_TAC-50 "McMillan TAC-50"), and [Anzio 20mm rifle](/wiki/Anzio_20mm_rifle "Anzio 20mm rifle"). Examples of designated marksman rifles include the [SVD](/wiki/SVD_(rifle) "SVD (rifle)"), [SR-25](/wiki/SR-25 "SR-25"), [Dragunov SVU](/wiki/Dragunov_SVU "Dragunov SVU"), [Marine Scout Sniper Rifle](/wiki/Marine_Scout_Sniper_Rifle "Marine Scout Sniper Rifle"), [Mk 14 Enhanced Battle Rifle](/wiki/Mk_14_Enhanced_Battle_Rifle "Mk 14 Enhanced Battle Rifle"), [M110 Semi-Automatic Sniper System](/wiki/M110_Semi-Automatic_Sniper_System "M110 Semi-Automatic Sniper System"), and [M110A1 Compact Semi-Automatic Sniper System](/wiki/M110A1_rifle "M110A1 rifle").

#### Automatic rifles

Main article: [Automatic rifle](/wiki/Automatic_rifle "Automatic rifle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/AVS-36_AM.068386_%282%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-AVS-36_AM.068386_%282%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:AVS-36_AM.068386_(2)_(cropped).jpg)

The [AVS-36](/wiki/AVS-36 "AVS-36"), an automatic rifle chambered in [7.62×54mmR](/wiki/7.62%C3%9754mmR "7.62×54mmR")

An automatic rifle is a magazine-fed rifle that is capable of automatic fire, including both [burst fire](/wiki/Burst_mode_(weapons) "Burst mode (weapons)") rifles like the [M16A4](/wiki/M16#M16A4 "M16") & [M4](/wiki/M4_carbine "M4 carbine") which fire 3-round bursts with each trigger pull, and [full auto](/wiki/Automatic_firearm "Automatic firearm") rifles like the [M16](/wiki/M16 "M16") & [M4A1](/wiki/M4_carbine#M4A1 "M4 carbine") which fire as long as the trigger is held down.[[53]](#cite_note-Full_auto-53) They include most assault rifles and battle rifles, but originated as their own category of rifles capable of automatic fire, as opposed to the bolt-action and semi-automatic rifles commonly issued to infantry at the time of their invention in the early 20th century. They usually have smaller magazine capacities than [machine guns](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun"); the French [Chauchat](/wiki/Chauchat "Chauchat") had a 20-round box magazine and the U.S. [M16](/wiki/M16 "M16") has a 30-round detachable magazine, while the [Hotchkiss Mle 1914 machine gun](/wiki/Hotchkiss_Mle_1914_machine_gun "Hotchkiss Mle 1914 machine gun"), the [French Army](/wiki/French_Army "French Army")'s standard machine gun at the time, was fed by a 250-round [ammunition belt](/wiki/Ammunition_belt "Ammunition belt").[[53]](#cite_note-Full_auto-53)

Though automatic rifles are sometimes considered to be their own category, they are also occasionally considered to be other types of firearms that postdated their invention, usually as [light machine guns](/wiki/Light_machine_gun "Light machine gun"). Automatic rifles are sometimes confused with machine guns or vice versa, or are defined as such by law; the [National Firearms Act](/wiki/National_Firearms_Act "National Firearms Act") (NFA) and Title II of the [Gun Control Act of 1968](/wiki/Gun_Control_Act_of_1968 "Gun Control Act of 1968") (GCA) define a "machine gun" in [*26 United States Code § 5845(b)*](/wiki/United_States_Code "United States Code") as "... any firearm which shoots ... automatically more than one shot, without manual reloading, by a single function of the trigger".[[54]](#cite_note-54) "Machine gun" is therefore largely synonymous with "automatic weapon" in American civilian parlance, covering all automatic firearms. In most jurisdictions, automatic rifles, as well as automatic firearms in general, are prohibited from civilian purchase or are at least heavily restricted. In the U.S. for instance, most automatic rifles are [Title II weapons](/wiki/Title_II_weapons "Title II weapons") under the NFA & GCA that are heavily regulated and require prior approval from the ATF, a special permit, and payment of a $200 tax;[[36]](#cite_note-NFA-36) in addition the [Hughes Act of 1986](/wiki/Firearm_Owners_Protection_Act "Firearm Owners Protection Act"), codified at *18 U.S.C. § 922(o)*, banned the transfer and possession of machine guns to civilians with the exception of machine guns registered prior to July 1, 1986,[[55]](#cite_note-55) which is why civilian-legal machine guns in the U.S. are incredibly expensive.[[56]](#cite_note-Hughes_Act-56)

Examples of automatic rifles include the [Cei-Rigotti](/wiki/Cei-Rigotti "Cei-Rigotti"), [Lewis gun](/wiki/Lewis_gun "Lewis gun"), [Fedorov Avtomat](/wiki/Fedorov_Avtomat "Fedorov Avtomat"), [M1918 Browning automatic rifle](/wiki/M1918_Browning_automatic_rifle "M1918 Browning automatic rifle"), [AK-47](/wiki/AK-47 "AK-47"), [M16](/wiki/M16 "M16"), [M4 carbine](/wiki/M4_carbine "M4 carbine"), and [H&K 416](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_HK416 "Heckler & Koch HK416").

#### Submachine guns

Main article: [Submachine gun](/wiki/Submachine_gun "Submachine gun")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Heckler_%26_Koch_MP5-1.jpg/250px-Heckler_%26_Koch_MP5-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heckler_%26_Koch_MP5-1.jpg)

The [Heckler & Koch MP5](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_MP5 "Heckler & Koch MP5"), a submachine gun chambered in [9×19mm Parabellum](/wiki/9%C3%9719mm_Parabellum "9×19mm Parabellum")

A submachine gun is a magazine-fed carbine chambered in a small-caliber [handgun cartridge](/wiki/Handgun_cartridge "Handgun cartridge") (such as [9×19mm Parabellum](/wiki/9%C3%9719mm_Parabellum "9×19mm Parabellum"), [.45 ACP](/wiki/.45_ACP ".45 ACP"), [.22 Long Rifle](/wiki/.22_Long_Rifle ".22 Long Rifle"), and [.40 S&W](/wiki/.40_S%26W ".40 S&W")).[[57]](#cite_note-SMG_v._MG-57) They cannot be considered machine guns due to their small-caliber, hence the prefix "[sub-](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/sub- "wiktionary:sub-")" to differentiate them from proper machine guns. Submachine guns are commonly associated with high rates of fire, automatic fire capabilities, and low recoil, though many submachine guns differentiate from this in various ways, such as having fairly low rates of fire or including burst and semi-automatic modes available through selective fire. Most submachine guns are the size of carbines and short-barreled rifles, and use similar configurations. Many are designed to take as little space as possible for use in close-quarters or for easy storage in vehicles and cases. Some submachine guns are designed and configured similar to pistols even down to size, and are thus occasionally classed as [machine pistols](/wiki/Machine_pistol "Machine pistol"), even if they are not actually a handgun (i.e. designed to require two hands to use).

Submachine guns are considered ideal for [close-quarters combat](/wiki/Close-quarters_combat "Close-quarters combat") and are cheap to mass-produce. They were very common in military service through much of the 20th century, but have since been superseded in most combat roles by rifles, carbines, and personal defense weapons due to their low effective range and poor penetration against most [body armor](/wiki/Body_armor "Body armor") developed since the late 20th century. However, they remain popular among special forces and police for their effectiveness in close-quarters and low likelihood to [overpenetrate](/wiki/Overpenetrating "Overpenetrating") targets.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

Examples of submachine guns include the [MP 18](/wiki/MP_18 "MP 18"), [MP 40](/wiki/MP_40 "MP 40"), [Thompson submachine gun](/wiki/Thompson_submachine_gun "Thompson submachine gun"), [M3 submachine gun](/wiki/M3_submachine_gun "M3 submachine gun"), [Uzi](/wiki/Uzi "Uzi"), [Heckler & Koch MP5](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_MP5 "Heckler & Koch MP5"), [Spectre M4](/wiki/Spectre_M4 "Spectre M4"), [Steyr TMP](/wiki/Steyr_TMP "Steyr TMP"), [Heckler & Koch UMP](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_UMP "Heckler & Koch UMP"), [PP-2000](/wiki/PP-2000 "PP-2000"), [KRISS Vector](/wiki/KRISS_Vector "KRISS Vector"), and [SIG MPX](/wiki/SIG_MPX "SIG MPX").

#### Personal defense weapons

Main article: [Personal defense weapon](/wiki/Personal_defense_weapon "Personal defense weapon")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/BundeswehrMP7.JPG/250px-BundeswehrMP7.JPG)](/wiki/File:BundeswehrMP7.JPG)

The [Heckler & Koch MP7](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_MP7 "Heckler & Koch MP7"), a personal defense weapon chambered in [HK 4.6×30mm](/wiki/HK_4.6%C3%9730mm "HK 4.6×30mm")

A personal defense weapon (PDW) is a light-weight, short, collapsible, folding rifle or submachine gun, chambered in either intermediate or rifle rounds, designed specifically for close-quarters protection.[[59]](#cite_note-SIG_PDW-59) A PDW is more compact than a rifle, but is more effective than a handgun at intermediate ranges around 100 yards (91 m) to 200 yd (180 m), and are capable of holding more ammunition than a handgun. Examples of [automatic](/wiki/Automatic_firearm "Automatic firearm") PDWs used by military and law enforcement are the [FN P90](/wiki/FN_P90 "FN P90") chambered in the [5.7×28mm](/wiki/5.7%C3%9728mm "5.7×28mm") intermediate cartridge, and the [Heckler & Koch MP7](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_MP7 "Heckler & Koch MP7") chambered for the [HK 4.6×30mm](/wiki/HK_4.6%C3%9730mm "HK 4.6×30mm") [armor-piercing](/wiki/Armor-piercing_bullet "Armor-piercing bullet") cartridge. An example of a [semi-automatic](/wiki/Semi-automatic_firearm "Semi-automatic firearm") civilian-legal PDW would be an [AR pistol](/wiki/AR-15%E2%80%93style_pistol "AR-15–style pistol"), which are essentially short-barreled AR-15s with an overall length under 26 in (66 cm) that use an arm brace instead of a buttstock, allowing it to be fired one-handed like a handgun; AR pistols can be chambered in pistol cartridges like [9x19mm](/wiki/9x19mm "9x19mm"), [.357 SIG](/wiki/.357_SIG ".357 SIG"), & [.40 S&W](/wiki/.40_S%26W ".40 S&W"), or rifle cartridges like the [5.56x45mm](/wiki/5.56x45mm "5.56x45mm") & [.300 Blackout](/wiki/.300_Blackout ".300 Blackout").[[60]](#cite_note-PDW-60)

Personal defense weapons were developed to provide [rear](/wiki/Rear_(military) "Rear (military)") and "second-line" personnel not otherwise armed with high-caliber firearms (vehicle and weapon crews, engineers, logistical personnel, etc.) with a method of effective self-defense against [skirmishers](/wiki/Skirmisher "Skirmisher") and [infiltrators](/wiki/Infiltration_tactics "Infiltration tactics") who cannot effectively be defeated by low-powered submachine guns and handguns, often the only firearms suitable for those personnel (while they could be issued rifles or carbines, those would become unnecessary burdens in their normal duties, during which the likelihood of hostility is fairly rare regardless, making their issuance questionable). PDWs were also designed for use by special forces teams as some use specialized ammunition designed to offer better penetration against modern body armor than traditional pistol rounds but with reduced recoil, such as the MP7, they also offer an advantage for missions when stealth and concealability are needed. PDWs are also used by law enforcement for situations when they don't want to appear conspicuously armed, such as undercover [DEA](/wiki/DEA "DEA") and [FBI](/wiki/FBI "FBI") agents or [Secret Service](/wiki/United_States_Secret_Service "United States Secret Service") agents on protection details, as a PDW can easily be concealed under a coat or jacket but still be rapidly deployed.[[60]](#cite_note-PDW-60)

Examples of personal defense weapons include the [FN P90](/wiki/FN_P90 "FN P90"), [Heckler & Koch MP7](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_MP7 "Heckler & Koch MP7"), [AAC Honey Badger](/wiki/AAC_Honey_Badger "AAC Honey Badger"), [ST Kinetics CPW](/wiki/ST_Kinetics_CPW "ST Kinetics CPW"), [SIG MCX](/wiki/SIG_MCX "SIG MCX"), and [SIG MPX](/wiki/SIG_MPX "SIG MPX").

#### Machine guns

Main article: [Machine gun](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/M60_machine_gun.jpg/250px-M60_machine_gun.jpg)](/wiki/File:M60_machine_gun.jpg)

The [M60](/wiki/M60_machine_gun "M60 machine gun"), a general-purpose machine gun chambered in [7.62×51mm NATO](/wiki/7.62%C3%9751mm_NATO "7.62×51mm NATO")

A machine gun is a fully-automatic firearm, chambered in intermediate or full-power rifle cartridges, designed to provide sustained automatic [direct fire](/wiki/Direct_fire "Direct fire") as opposed to the semi-automatic or [burst fire](/wiki/Burst_fire "Burst fire") of standard rifles.[[53]](#cite_note-Full_auto-53) They are commonly associated with being [belt-fed](/wiki/Belt-fed "Belt-fed"), though many machine guns are also fed by box, drum, pan, or hopper magazines. They generally have a high [rate of fire](/wiki/Rate_of_fire "Rate of fire") and a large ammunition capacity, and are often used for [suppressive fire](/wiki/Suppressive_fire "Suppressive fire") to support infantry advances or defend positions from enemy assaults.[[61]](#cite_note-LOC_machine_gun-61) Owing to their versatility and firepower, they are also commonly installed on [military vehicles](/wiki/Military_vehicle "Military vehicle") and [military aircraft](/wiki/Military_aircraft "Military aircraft"), either as main or ancillary weapons.[[62]](#cite_note-USNI_machine_gun-62) Many machine guns are individual-served and can be operated by a single soldier, though some are [crew-served weapons](/wiki/Crew-served_weapon "Crew-served weapon") that require a dedicated crew of soldiers to operate, usually between two and six soldiers depending on the machine gun's operation and the crew members' roles (ammunition bearers, spotters, etc.).[[63]](#cite_note-63)

Machine guns can be divided into three categories: [light machine guns](/wiki/Light_machine_gun "Light machine gun"), individual-served machine guns of an [intermediate cartridge](/wiki/Intermediate_cartridge "Intermediate cartridge") that are usually magazine-fed; [medium machine guns](/wiki/Medium_machine_gun "Medium machine gun"), belt-fed machine guns of a full-power caliber and a certain weight that can be operated by an individual but tend to work best with a crew; and [heavy machine guns](/wiki/Heavy_machine_gun "Heavy machine gun"), machine guns that are too large and heavy to be carried and are thus [mounted](/wiki/Weapon_mount "Weapon mount") to something (like a [tripod](/wiki/Tripod "Tripod") or [military vehicle](/wiki/Military_vehicle "Military vehicle")), and require a crew to operate.[[61]](#cite_note-LOC_machine_gun-61) A [general-purpose machine gun](/wiki/General-purpose_machine_gun "General-purpose machine gun") combines these categories under a single flexible machine gun platform, often one that is most suitable as a light or medium machine gun but fares well as a heavy machine gun. A closely related concept is the [squad automatic weapon](/wiki/Squad_automatic_weapon "Squad automatic weapon"), a portable light machine gun or even a modified rifle that is designed and fielded to provide a [squad](/wiki/Squad "Squad") with rapid [direct fire](/wiki/Direct_fire "Direct fire").[[64]](#cite_note-64)

Examples of machine guns include the [Maxim gun](/wiki/Maxim_gun "Maxim gun"), [M2 Browning](/wiki/M2_Browning "M2 Browning"), [Bren light machine gun](/wiki/Bren_light_machine_gun "Bren light machine gun"), [MG 42](/wiki/MG_42 "MG 42"), [PK machine gun](/wiki/PK_machine_gun "PK machine gun"), [FN MAG](/wiki/FN_MAG "FN MAG"), [M249 light machine gun](/wiki/M249_light_machine_gun "M249 light machine gun"), [RPK](/wiki/RPK "RPK"), [IWI Negev](/wiki/IWI_Negev "IWI Negev"), and [M134 Minigun](/wiki/M134_Minigun "M134 Minigun").

## Action

Main article: [Action (firearms)](/wiki/Action_(firearms) "Action (firearms)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/AR-15_and_Remington_Model_870.jpg/250px-AR-15_and_Remington_Model_870.jpg)](/wiki/File:AR-15_and_Remington_Model_870.jpg)

The semi-automatic [Colt AR-15](/wiki/Colt_AR-15 "Colt AR-15") (top) and the pump action [Remington Model 870](/wiki/Remington_Model_870 "Remington Model 870") (bottom); these weapons' actions are common for their respective types.

Types aside, firearms are also categorized by their "action", which describes their loading, firing, and unloading cycle.

### Manual

*Manual action* or *manual operation* is essentially any type of firearm action that is loaded, and usually also fired, one cartridge at a time by the user, rather than automatically. Manual action firearms can be divided into two basic categories: [single-shot](/wiki/Single-shot "Single-shot") firearms that can only be fired once per barrel before it must be reloaded or charged via an external mechanism or series of steps; and [repeating](/wiki/Repeating_firearm "Repeating firearm") firearms that can be fired multiple times per barrel, but can only be fired once with each subsequent pull of the trigger or ignite, and the firearm's action must be reloaded or charged via an internal mechanism between trigger pulls.[[65]](#cite_note-IPSC_Manual-65)

Types of manual actions include lever action, bolt action, and pump action.

#### Break action

*Break action* or *hinge action* firearms open like a door on a hinge, providing access to the [breech](/wiki/Chamber_(firearms) "Chamber (firearms)") to load or unload the firearm, once the gun has been fired the action can then be opened to unload the spent shell(s) and reload. This type of action is most common with shotguns, but it is also seen with certain types of rifles such as [double rifles](/wiki/Double_rifles "Double rifles") and certain types of pistols. Break-action firearms commonly use either a single barrel, meaning only one shot can be fired before reloading, or use two barrels like with [double-barreled](/wiki/Double-barreled_shotgun "Double-barreled shotgun") or over-under shotgun and rifles.[[66]](#cite_note-DD_action_types-66)

Examples of break-action single barrel shotguns include the [Winchester Model 37](/wiki/Winchester_Model_37 "Winchester Model 37"), a double-barrel example is the [Stoeger Coach Gun](/wiki/Stoeger_Coach_Gun "Stoeger Coach Gun"), and a break-action rifle example would be the high end [Holland & Holland](/wiki/Holland_%26_Holland "Holland & Holland") double rifle, such as the [.500 Nitro Express](/wiki/.500_Nitro_Express ".500 Nitro Express").

#### Lever action

Main article: [Lever action](/wiki/Lever_action "Lever action")

*Lever action* is a repeating action that is operated by using a [cocking handle](/wiki/Cocking_handle "Cocking handle") (the "lever") located around the [trigger guard](/wiki/Trigger_guard "Trigger guard") area (often incorporating it) that is pulled down then back up to move the [bolt](/wiki/Bolt_(firearms) "Bolt (firearms)") via internal [linkages](/wiki/Linkage_(mechanical) "Linkage (mechanical)"). As the bolt moves back it pushes the [hammer](/wiki/Hammer_(firearms) "Hammer (firearms)") back (most lever-action firearms use a hammer) into the cocked position, or in the case of a hammerless model it resets the [firing pin](/wiki/Firing_pin "Firing pin") mechanism. This also causes the bolt to expel the old [cartridge](/wiki/Cartridge_(firearms) "Cartridge (firearms)") and a fresh round to pop out of the [magazine](/wiki/Magazine_(firearms)#Tubular "Magazine (firearms)") tube or box (most lever-action rifles use a tubular magazine), with the follower raising the round up into position, then as the lever is pushed back up the bolt closes and pushes the fresh round into the [chamber](/wiki/Chamber_(firearms) "Chamber (firearms)"). Most lever-action firearms are rifles, but there are a few types of [lever-action shotguns](/wiki/Lever_action#Shotguns "Lever action").[[66]](#cite_note-DD_action_types-66)

Examples of lever-action rifles include the [Henry rifle](/wiki/Henry_rifle "Henry rifle"), [Winchester Model 1894](/wiki/Winchester_Model_1894 "Winchester Model 1894"), & [Marlin Model 1895](/wiki/Marlin_Model_1895 "Marlin Model 1895").

#### Revolving action

Main article: [Revolver](/wiki/Revolver "Revolver")

*Revolving action* is a type of firearm action primarily found on handguns known as *revolvers*, but there are a few types of revolving rifles and shotguns. Revolvers are so named because cartridges are loaded into a cylinder, the cylinder has separate chambers for each cartridge (the most common size is six), as the revolver is fired the cylinder rotates (or "revolves") aligning each of the chambers with the barrel. There are two types of revolvers: single-action and double-action. With [single-action](/wiki/Trigger_(firearms)#Single-action "Trigger (firearms)") revolvers, the hammer must be manually cocked back by the operator each time the gun is fired to rotate the cylinder. With [double-action](/wiki/Trigger_(firearms)#Double-action/single-action "Trigger (firearms)") revolvers, pulling the trigger rotates the cylinder, cocks the hammer back, and releases the hammer all in one motion, allowing for a faster rate-of-fire; however the revolver can also be fired single-action style by manually cocking the hammer, this makes the trigger pull much lighter and improves accuracy. Some modern revolvers are known as [double-action only](/wiki/Trigger_(firearms)#Double-action_only "Trigger (firearms)"), instead of having an external hammer they use an internal [striker](/wiki/Firing_pin#Striker "Firing pin") mechanism, as the trigger is pulled it rotates the cylinder and resets the striker's firing pin, as such it can only be fired double-action style.[[66]](#cite_note-DD_action_types-66)

Examples of revolvers include the [Colt Single Action Army](/wiki/Colt_Single_Action_Army "Colt Single Action Army"), [Colt M1889](/wiki/Colt_M1889 "Colt M1889") (early double-action), [Colt Python](/wiki/Colt_Python "Colt Python") (modern double-action), [Ruger Single-Six](/wiki/Ruger_Single-Six "Ruger Single-Six") (modern single-action), & [Ruger LCR](/wiki/Ruger_LCR "Ruger LCR") (double-action only).

#### Bolt action

Main article: [Bolt action](/wiki/Bolt_action "Bolt action")

*Bolt action* is a repeating (and rarely single-shot) action that is operated by directly manipulating the bolt via a [bolt handle](/wiki/Cocking_handle "Cocking handle"). The bolt is unlocked from the [receiver](/wiki/Receiver_(firearms) "Receiver (firearms)"), then pulled back to open the [breech](/wiki/Breech-loading_weapon "Breech-loading weapon"), ejecting a cartridge, and cocking the [striker](/wiki/Firing_pin "Firing pin") and engaging it against the [sear](/wiki/Sear_(firearm) "Sear (firearm)"); when the bolt is returned to the forward position, a new cartridge, if loaded, is pushed out of the magazine and into the barrel [chamber](/wiki/Chamber_(firearms) "Chamber (firearms)"), and the breech is re-locked. Most bolt-action firearms are rifles, but a few are shotguns.[[66]](#cite_note-DD_action_types-66) Two designs of bolt action exist: *rotating bolt*, where the bolt must be axially rotated to unlock and lock the receiver; and *straight pull*, which does not require the bolt to be rotated, simplifying the bolt action mechanism and allowing for a greater rate of fire.

Examples of bolt-action rifles include the [Winchester Model 70](/wiki/Winchester_Model_70 "Winchester Model 70"), [Remington Model 700](/wiki/Remington_Model_700 "Remington Model 700"), [Ruger M77](/wiki/Ruger_M77 "Ruger M77"), & [Savage Model 110](/wiki/Savage_Model_110 "Savage Model 110").

#### Pump action

Main article: [Pump action](/wiki/Pump_action "Pump action")

*Pump action* or *slide action* is a repeating action that is operated by moving a sliding [handguard](/wiki/Handguard "Handguard") (the "pump") on the gun's [forestock](/wiki/Forestock "Forestock") rearward (frontward on some models), ejecting any spent cartridges and cocking the [hammer](/wiki/Hammer_(firearms) "Hammer (firearms)") or striker, then moving the handguard forward to load a new cartridge into the chamber.[[67]](#cite_note-67) It is most common on shotguns, though pump action rifles and grenade launchers also exist.[[66]](#cite_note-DD_action_types-66)

Examples of pump-action firearms include the [Remington Model 870](/wiki/Remington_Model_870 "Remington Model 870"), [Mossberg 500](/wiki/Mossberg_500 "Mossberg 500"), & [Winchester Model 1200](/wiki/Winchester_Model_1200 "Winchester Model 1200") shotguns and [Remington Model 7600](/wiki/Remington_Model_7600 "Remington Model 7600") rifle.

### Automatic

*Automatic*, *autoloading* or *self-loading* firearms use the energy, force, and/or gases generated by the cartridge being discharged to cycle the action. There are three primary methods of operation for automatic firearms: blowback, gas-operated, and recoil-operated. [Blowback](/wiki/Blowback_(firearms) "Blowback (firearms)") firearms use the energy from the motion of the cartridge case as it is pushed to the rear by expanding gas created by the ignition of the gunpowder in the cartridge. [Gas-operated](/wiki/Gas-operated_reloading "Gas-operated reloading") firearms use a portion of high-pressure gas from the cartridge being fired to cycle the action, there are two main types of gas-operated firearms: [direct impingement](/wiki/Direct_impingement "Direct impingement") in which the gas is captured by a gas block then directed back into the action forcing the bolt backwards into a spring, [piston operated](/wiki/Gas-operated_reloading#Long-stroke "Gas-operated reloading") guns use the gas in the gas block to force a piston above the barrel backwards to cycle the bolt. [Recoil-operated](/wiki/Recoil_operation "Recoil operation") firearms use the force of the recoil to cycle the action, in recoil-operated firearms the barrel is free-floating and locked together with the slide, the barrel & slide are then moved backwards by the recoil, ejecting the spent case and loading a fresh one from the magazine, with a spring propelling them back into the forward position.[[22]](#cite_note-USCCA-22)

An example of a blowback firearm is the [Walther PPK](/wiki/Walther_PPK "Walther PPK"). Examples of gas-operated guns are the [M16](/wiki/M16 "M16") & [AR-15](/wiki/AR-15_style_rifle "AR-15 style rifle") (direct impingement) and [AK-47](/wiki/AK-47 "AK-47") (piston operated). An example of a recoil operated firearm is a [Glock](/wiki/Glock "Glock") pistol.

#### Semi-automatic

Main article: [Semi-automatic firearm](/wiki/Semi-automatic_firearm "Semi-automatic firearm")

*Semi-automatic* is a firearm action that only requires the operator to manually load the first round, usually by either pulling a charging handle (in the case of an AR-style rifle) or pulling back (racking) the slide (in the case of a pistol), and moving the [safety](/wiki/Safety_(firearms) "Safety (firearms)") into the "FIRE" position (not all firearms use a safety switch). After the discharge of the first round, the firearm automatically performs the feeding and ignition procedures necessary to prepare the firearm for a subsequent discharge. Semi-automatic firearms only discharge once with each trigger actuation, and the trigger must be actuated again to fire another cartridge.[[66]](#cite_note-DD_action_types-66)

Examples of semi-automatic handguns include the [Colt 1911](/wiki/Colt_1911 "Colt 1911") & [Glock](/wiki/Glock "Glock"), semi-automatic rifles include the [AR-15](/wiki/AR-15%E2%80%93style_rifle "AR-15–style rifle"), [Ruger 10/22](/wiki/Ruger_10/22 "Ruger 10/22"), [Ruger Mini-14](/wiki/Ruger_Mini-14 "Ruger Mini-14"), & [Springfield Armory M1A](/wiki/Springfield_Armory_M1A "Springfield Armory M1A"), and semi-automatic shotguns include the [Benelli Super Black Eagle](/wiki/Benelli_Armi "Benelli Armi"), [Mossberg 940 Pro](/wiki/O.F._Mossberg_%26_Sons "O.F. Mossberg & Sons") & [Remington Model 1100](/wiki/Remington_Model_1100 "Remington Model 1100").

#### Automatic

Main article: [Automatic firearm](/wiki/Automatic_firearm "Automatic firearm")

*Automatic* is a firearm action that uses the same automated action cycling as semi-automatic, but continues to do so for as long as the trigger is actuated, until the trigger is let go of or the firearm is depleted of available ammunition. The excess energy released from a discharged cartridge is used to load a new cartridge into the chamber, then igniting the [propellant](/wiki/Propellant "Propellant") and discharging said new cartridge by delivering a hammer or striker impact on the [primer](/wiki/Primer_(firearms) "Primer (firearms)"). Automatic firearms are further defined by the type of [cycling principles](/wiki/Action_(firearms)#Autoloading_operation "Action (firearms)") used, such as [recoil operation](/wiki/Recoil_operation "Recoil operation") (uses energy from the [recoil](/wiki/Recoil "Recoil") to cycle the action), [blowback](/wiki/Blowback_(firearms) "Blowback (firearms)") (uses energy from the cartridge case as it is pushed by expanding gas), [blow forward](/wiki/Blow_forward "Blow forward") (use propellant gas pressure to open the breech), or [gas operation](/wiki/Gas_operation "Gas operation") (uses high-pressure gas from a fired cartridge to dispose of the spent case and load a new cartridge).[[53]](#cite_note-Full_auto-53)

Examples of automatic firearms include the [Lewis gun](/wiki/Lewis_gun "Lewis gun"), [M2 Browning](/wiki/M2_Browning "M2 Browning"), & [M60](/wiki/M60_machine_gun "M60 machine gun").

#### Burst

Main article: [Burst mode (weapons)](/wiki/Burst_mode_(weapons) "Burst mode (weapons)")

*Burst* is a fire mode of some automatic firearms that fires a predetermined amount of rounds—usually two or three—in the same manner as automatic fire. Depending on the firearm, a single trigger actuation may fire the full burst of rounds, or it must be depressed for the entire discharge, with a single pull of the trigger firing a single round or an incomplete burst. Most firearms with burst capabilities have it as a fire mode secondary to semi-automatic or full-automatic.[[53]](#cite_note-Full_auto-53) In most countries, including the United States, burst-fire weapons are considered to be a type of machine gun and are regulated as such, in the U.S. any firearm that fires more than one round with a single trigger actuation is a machine gun as defined by the [National Firearms Act of 1934](/wiki/National_Firearms_Act_of_1934 "National Firearms Act of 1934") and Title II of the [Gun Control Act of 1968](/wiki/Gun_Control_Act_of_1968 "Gun Control Act of 1968").[[36]](#cite_note-NFA-36)[[68]](#cite_note-68)

Examples of burst fire weapons include the [M16A4](/wiki/M16_rifle#M16A4 "M16 rifle") & [M4 carbine](/wiki/M4_carbine "M4 carbine").

#### Selective fire

Main article: [Selective fire](/wiki/Selective_fire "Selective fire")

*Selective fire* or *select fire* is the capability of a firearm to have its fire mode adjusted between semi-automatic, burst, or automatic. The modes are chosen by means of a [fire mode selector](/wiki/Fire_mode_selector "Fire mode selector"), which varies depending on the weapon's design. The presence of selective-fire modes on firearms allows more efficient use of ammunition for specific tactical needs, either precision-aimed or [suppressive fire](/wiki/Suppressive_fire "Suppressive fire"). Selective fire is most commonly found on assault rifles and submachine guns.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Examples of selective fire weapons include the [M16A1](/wiki/M16_rifle#M16A1 "M16 rifle") (SEMI/FULL), [M16A2](/wiki/M16_rifle#M16A2 "M16 rifle") (SEMI/BURST), [M4](/wiki/M4_carbine "M4 carbine") (SEMI/BURST), [M4A1](/wiki/M4_carbine#M4A1 "M4 carbine") (SEMI/FULL), & [MP5A4](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_MP5 "Heckler & Koch MP5") (SEMI/BURST/FULL).

## Use as a blunt weapon

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/U.S._Air_Force_Buttstroke.jpg/250px-U.S._Air_Force_Buttstroke.jpg)](/wiki/File:U.S._Air_Force_Buttstroke.jpg)

A [United States Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force") trainee [buttstroking](/wiki/Buttstroking "Buttstroking") a target dummy with an unloaded M16 rifle

Firearms can be used as [blunt weapons](/wiki/Blunt_instrument "Blunt instrument"), for instance to conserve limited ammunition or when [ammunition](/wiki/Ammunition "Ammunition") has run out entirely.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

New recruits of the [Israel Defense Forces](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces") undergo training on the safe practice of using the M16 rifle as a blunt weapon, mainly so that in close-quarter fighting, the weapon cannot be pulled away from them. Other training includes the recruit learning how to jab parts of the body with the [muzzle](/wiki/Muzzle_(firearm) "Muzzle (firearm)") and using the [butt stock](/wiki/Stock_(firearm) "Stock (firearm)") as a weapon.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

[Forensic medicine](/wiki/Forensic_medicine "Forensic medicine") recognizes evidence for various types of [blunt-force injuries](/wiki/Blunt-force_injuries "Blunt-force injuries") produced by firearms. For example, "pistol-whipping" typically leaves semicircular or triangular lacerations of skin produced by the butt of a pistol.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

In armed robberies, beating the victims with firearms is a more common way to complete the robbery, rather than shooting or stabbing them.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

Examples include:

* [Buttstroking](/wiki/Buttstroking "Buttstroking"), striking with the [butt stock](/wiki/Stock_(firearm) "Stock (firearm)") of a firearm.[[74]](#cite_note-74)
* [Pistol-whipping](/wiki/Pistol-whipping "Pistol-whipping"), striking someone with a [handgun](/wiki/Handgun "Handgun").[[75]](#cite_note-75)
* Striking with the [muzzle](/wiki/Gun_barrel "Gun barrel") end of a firearm without a [bayonet](/wiki/Bayonet "Bayonet") attached.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

## History

Main article: [History of the firearm](/wiki/History_of_the_firearm "History of the firearm")

See also: [History of gunpowder](/wiki/History_of_gunpowder "History of gunpowder")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Yuan_chinese_gun.jpg/250px-Yuan_chinese_gun.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yuan_chinese_gun.jpg)

[Hand cannon](/wiki/Hand_cannon "Hand cannon") from the Chinese [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty") (1271–1368)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/%E6%A2%A8%E8%8A%B1%E9%8E%97.jpg/250px-%E6%A2%A8%E8%8A%B1%E9%8E%97.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E6%A2%A8%E8%8A%B1%E9%8E%97.jpg)

Chinese [fire lance](/wiki/Fire_lance "Fire lance"), from the *[Huolongjing](/wiki/Huolongjing "Huolongjing")*

The first firearms were invented in 10th century [China](/wiki/History_of_science_and_technology_in_China "History of science and technology in China") when the man-portable [fire lance](/wiki/Fire_lance "Fire lance") (a bamboo or metal tube that could shoot ignited [gunpowder](/wiki/Gunpowder "Gunpowder")) was combined with projectiles such as scrap metal, broken porcelain, or darts/arrows.[[4]](#cite_note-Helaine-4)[[77]](#cite_note-77) Over the centuries in China, several improvements were made to the fire lances and slowly transformed it into the metal-barreled hand cannon (*[huochong](/wiki/Huochong "Huochong")*).[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham198610-78)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Such improvements included increasing the proportion of saltpeter in gunpowder formulas to enhance explosive force.[[79]](#cite_note-:1-79) To withstand the greater explosive force produced, barrels were constructed from metal rather than bamboo or wood.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChase200331–32-80) At the same time, loose shrapnel loads were replaced with solid projectiles sized to fit the barrel more closely.[[79]](#cite_note-:1-79) Together, these improvements resulted in the hand cannon, featuring a metal barrel, high-nitrate gunpowder, and a fitted projectile.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAndrade201652-5)[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeedham198610-78)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/%E9%BB%91%E9%BE%8D%E6%B1%9F%E7%81%AB%E9%8A%83.jpg/250px-%E9%BB%91%E9%BE%8D%E6%B1%9F%E7%81%AB%E9%8A%83.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E9%BB%91%E9%BE%8D%E6%B1%9F%E7%81%AB%E9%8A%83.jpg)

[Heilongjiang Hand Cannon](/wiki/Heilongjiang_hand_cannon "Heilongjiang hand cannon"), dated to 1288 CE, the world's oldest surviving firearm. It includes a touch hole and a gunpowder chamber. The gunpowder would be ignited using a fuse.

An early depiction of a firearm is a sculpture from a cave in [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan"), China. The sculpture dates to the 12th century[[81]](#cite_note-81) and represents a figure carrying a vase-shaped [bombard](/wiki/Bombard_(weapon) "Bombard (weapon)"), with flames and a [cannonball](/wiki/Round_shot "Round shot") coming out of it.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChase200331–32-80) The oldest surviving gun, the [Heilongjiang hand cannon](/wiki/Heilongjiang_hand_cannon "Heilongjiang hand cannon"), was a [hand cannon](/wiki/Hand_cannon "Hand cannon") made of bronze, dating to 1288 because it was discovered at a site in modern-day [Acheng District](/wiki/Acheng_District "Acheng District"), [Heilongjiang](/wiki/Heilongjiang "Heilongjiang"), China, where the *Yuan Shi([History of Yuan](/wiki/History_of_Yuan "History of Yuan"))* records that battles were fought at that time.[[82]](#cite_note-needham_volume_5_part_7_293_294-82) It describes a Jurchen commander by the name of Li Ting who led a group of soldiers equipped with hand cannons in 1288, to put down a rebellion in the region. The *History of Yuan* reports that the hand cannons not only "caused great damage," but also caused "such confusion that the enemy soldiers attacked and killed each other."[[83]](#cite_note-83) The firearm had a 17.5 cm (6.9 in) barrel of a 2.5 cm (1 in) diameter, a 6.6 cm (2.6 in) chamber for the gunpowder and a socket for the firearm's handle. It is 34 centimetres (13 in) long and 3.54 kilograms (7.8 lb) without the handle, which would have been made of wood.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChase200332-84)

The Arabs and [Mamluks](/wiki/Mamluk "Mamluk") had firearms in the late-13th century.[[85]](#cite_note-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87) Europeans obtained firearms in the 14th century.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChase20031-88) The Koreans adopted firearms from the Chinese in the 14th century. The Iranians (first [Aq Qoyunlu](/wiki/Aq_Qoyunlu "Aq Qoyunlu") and [Safavids](/wiki/Safavids "Safavids")) and Indians (first [Mughals](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire")) all got them no later than the 15th century, from the Ottoman Turks. The people of the [Nusantara](/wiki/Nusantara_(archipelago) "Nusantara (archipelago)") archipelago of Southeast Asia used the [long arquebus](/wiki/Java_arquebus "Java arquebus") at least by the last quarter of the 15th century.[[89]](#cite_note-Crawfurd-1856-89): 23

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Satinggar_Malay.jpg/250px-Satinggar_Malay.jpg)](/wiki/File:Satinggar_Malay.jpg)

The [istinggar](/wiki/Istinggar "Istinggar"), a result of Indo-Portuguese gun-making traditions

Even though the knowledge of making gunpowder-based weapons in the [Nusantara](/wiki/Nusantara_(archipelago) "Nusantara (archipelago)") archipelago had been known after the failed [Mongol invasion of Java](/wiki/Mongol_invasion_of_Java "Mongol invasion of Java") (1293), and the predecessor of firearms, the [pole gun](/wiki/Hand_cannon "Hand cannon") ([bedil tombak](/wiki/Bedil_tombak "Bedil tombak")), was recorded as being used by Java in 1413,[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-Manguin-1976-91): 245 the knowledge of making "true" firearms came much later, after the middle of 15th century. It was brought by the [Islamic](/wiki/Islam "Islam") nations of West Asia, most probably the [Arabs](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs").
